one_line_takeaway: MUSE unifies multimodal payload generation, multi-turn jailbreak orchestration, and a five-level LLM-judge into one reproducible platform, and its ITMS ablation shows that near-perfectly-aligned multimodal LLMs collapse to 90-100% multi-turn attack success, with modality-switching acting as a convergence accelerator whose direction of effect (helps vs. hurts the attacker) is model-family-specific rather than universal.
key_finding: (1) All six models are near-perfectly aligned under single-turn pressure (90-100% refusal rate across text/audio/image/video, Table 1), but multi-turn attacks shatter this: Crescendo achieves 90-98% Hard ASR across all six models and PAIR reaches 96-100% on five of six (Table 2); the exception is PAIR vs. Claude Sonnet 4 (60% Hard ASR, with a 26-point gray-zone-width gap to Soft ASR indicating partial rather than complete disclosure). Violent Durian shows the widest cross-model variance (2% on Claude Sonnet 4 vs. 86% on Qwen2.5-Omni). (2) ITMS does not uniformly raise final ASR on already-saturated baselines (mixed deltas, e.g., Gemini 2.5 Flash +4, Qwen2.5-Omni -8 for ITMS-Crescendo) but accelerates convergence: ITMS-Crescendo reaches success in fewer turns for 4/6 models (e.g. Claude 3.0->2.6 turns, Qwen2.5 4.2->3.6 turns, Table 4), and where the text-only baseline has headroom, ITMS-VD raises Qwen2.5-Omni Hard ASR from 86% to 100% while cutting mean turns-to-success from 3.0 to 2.1. A turn-level mechanism: at turn 1, ITMS-Crescendo shows higher refusal than base Crescendo (86.0% vs 81.0%), but after the first modality switch at turn 2, refusal drops sharply (59.7% vs 66.8%) and Partial Compliance rises (32.7% vs 27.1%) -- a reversal absent in the text-only baseline. (3) The ITMS ablation (Table 3) shows the direction of the modality effect is model-family-specific, not universal: for Gemini models, non-text modalities raise Hard ASR 2-6 points above text baseline; for Qwen models the direction reverses, with non-text modalities consistently lowering ASR (sharpest drop image-only Qwen2.5-Omni, delta = -14). (4) Fraud is the most vulnerable harm category across all five strategies; Drugs and Weapons are the most resistant (Figure 3).
core_contribution: MUSE is an open-source, run-centric, browser-based platform (client-server, FastAPI backend) that unifies three components that prior tools kept separate: (1) automatic cross-modal payload generation converting attacker text into audio (TTS), image (PIL-rendered text on canvas), and video (FFmpeg composition of audio+image), (2) three multi-turn jailbreak attack algorithms (Crescendo, PAIR, Violent Durian) exposed through a common interface with provider-agnostic routing to six models across four providers (OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, Qwen), and (3) an LLM-judge with a five-level safety taxonomy (Compliance, Partial Compliance, Indirect Refusal, Direct Refusal, Non-Responsive) producing a dual-metric Hard ASR / Soft ASR framework. The paper's own novel methodological contribution on top of the platform is Inter-Turn Modality Switching (ITMS), which augments Crescendo and Violent Durian with per-turn modality rotation (cycling through the intersection of user-requested and model-supported modalities) to test whether refusal behavior generalizes across modality boundaries within a single multi-turn conversation. The authors are explicit that Crescendo, PAIR, Violent Durian, and the five-level/StrongREJECT-style taxonomy idea are adopted from prior work (Russinovich et al. 2024; Chao et al. 2023; AI Verify Foundation 2024; Souly et al. 2024) -- MUSE's contributions are the unified run-centric integration architecture and the ITMS extension.
threat_model: Attacker with only API/chat access to a target multimodal LLM (black-box), using an auxiliary attacker LLM (GPT-4o at temperature 0.9) to generate and iteratively rewrite adversarial prompts across up to 10 turns, optionally converting each turn's payload into a different modality (text/audio/image/video) via TTS/image-rendering/video-composition before delivery. Goal is to elicit Compliance (actionable harmful capability transfer) on 50 harmful goals drawn from AdvBench, spanning five categories: weapons, controlled substances, malware, biological threats, and fraud/social engineering. No white-box access, gradients, or model weights are assumed; the attack surface is purely the conversational/multimodal input interface exposed by provider APIs.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: GPT-4o and Claude Sonnet 4 were tested only on text and image because GPT-4o's audio input requires a separate Realtime API not used in the pipeline, and Claude Sonnet 4's Messages API does not accept audio -- so cross-modal comparisons are incomplete for these two models. Only 50 harmful goals from AdvBench across 5 categories were used, and GPT-4o served as both attacker and judge (single-model judge dependency, though partially mitigated by the 93%-agreement human validation on 100 sampled runs). Video modality was excluded from the ITMS ablation study specifically to avoid synthesis latency, leaving the ablation's disentangling of individual-modality vs. switching effects untested for video. The paper explicitly lists future work as supporting locally deployed open-source models, expanding ITMS to native video rotation, and validating the five-level judge against a larger/more systematic human-annotation study (only 100 runs were checked in this version).

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023) -- 50 harmful goals sampled evenly across 5 categories (weapons, controlled substances, malware, biological threats, fraud/social engineering), rephrased as direct capability requests deliverable across all supported modalities
quantitative_results: Single-turn baseline refusal rate: 90-100% across all six models and all tested modalities (Table 1)

Main experiment Hard ASR: Crescendo 90-98% across all six models; PAIR 96-100% on five of six models (exception: 60% on Claude Sonnet 4, GZW=26pp); Violent Durian ranges 2% (Claude Sonnet 4) to 86% (Qwen2.5-Omni)
ITMS-Crescendo vs Crescendo Hard ASR deltas: Gemini 2.5 Flash +4, Qwen2.5-Omni -8 (mixed, on saturated baselines)
ITMS-VD vs Violent Durian on Qwen2.5-Omni: Hard ASR 86% -> 100%, mean turns-to-success 3.0 -> 2.1
ITMS-Crescendo turns-to-success improves for 4/6 models: Claude Sonnet 4 3.0->2.6, Qwen2.5-Omni 4.2->3.6 (Table 4)
Turn-1 vs turn-2 refusal/partial-compliance shift for ITMS-Crescendo: turn1 refusal 86.0% (vs 81.0% base Crescendo); turn2 refusal 59.7% (vs 66.8% base), Partial Compliance 32.7% (vs 27.1% base)

ITMS modality ablation Hard ASR deltas vs text baseline (Table 3): Gemini 2.5 Flash +2 to +6 across non-text configs; Gemini 3 Flash 0 to +2; Qwen2.5-Omni -4 to -14 (worst: image-only, delta=-14); Qwen3-Omni -2 to -6
Human validation: 93% agreement between GPT-4o automated judge and human annotator on 100 manually re-labeled runs; no clear refusals mislabeled as Compliance
Total experimental scale: ~3,700 red-teaming runs (1,200 single-turn baseline + 1,500 main experiment + 1,000 ITMS ablation)

Harm-category vulnerability (Figure 3, aggregated across models): Fraud most vulnerable across all 5 strategies (e.g., Crescendo 100%, ITMS-VD 98%); Drugs and Weapons most resistant (e.g., Violent Durian: Weapons 33%, Drugs 38%)
baselines_compared: Six target models: Qwen3-Omni, Qwen2.5-Omni, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 3 Flash Preview, GPT-4o (text+image only), Claude Sonnet 4 (text+image only)
